message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  As an essential enzyme to SARS-CoV-2, main protease (M
                  <jats:sup>Pro</jats:sup>
                  ) is a viable target to develop antivirals for the treatment of COVID-19. By varying chemical compositions at both P2 and P3 sites and the
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  -terminal protection group, we synthesized a series of M
                  <jats:sup>Pro</jats:sup>
                  inhibitors that contain
                  <jats:italic>β</jats:italic>
                  -(S-2-oxopyrrolidin-3-yl)-alaninal at the P1 site. These inhibitors have a large variation of determined IC
                  <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                  values that range from 4.8 to 650 nM. The determined IC
                  <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                  values reveal that relatively small side chains at both P2 and P3 sites are favorable for achieving high
                  <jats:italic>in vitro</jats:italic>
                  M
                  <jats:sup>Pro</jats:sup>
                  inhibition potency, the P3 site is tolerable toward unnatural amino acids with two alkyl substituents on the
                  <jats:italic>α</jats:italic>
                  -carbon, and the inhibition potency is sensitive toward the
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  -terminal protection group. X-ray crystal structures of M
                  <jats:sup>Pro</jats:sup>
                  bound with 16 inhibitors were determined. All structures show similar binding patterns of inhibitors at the M
                  <jats:sup>Pro</jats:sup>
                  active site. A covalent interaction between the active site cysteine and a bound inhibitor was observed in all structures. In M
                  <jats:sup>Pro</jats:sup>
                  , large structural variations were observed on residues N142 and Q189. All inhibitors were also characterized on their inhibition of M
                  <jats:sup>Pro</jats:sup>
                  in 293T cells, which revealed their
                  <jats:italic>in cellulo</jats:italic>
                  potency that is drastically different from their
                  <jats:italic>in vitro</jats:italic>
                  enzyme inhibition potency. Inhibitors that showed high
                  <jats:italic>in cellulo</jats:italic>
                  potency all contain
                  <jats:italic>O</jats:italic>
                  -
                  <jats:italic>tert</jats:italic>
                  -butyl-threonine at the P3 site. Based on the current and a previous study, we conclude that
                  <jats:italic>O</jats:italic>
                  -
                  <jats:italic>tert</jats:italic>
                  -butyl-threonine at the P3 site is a key component to achieve high cellular and antiviral potency for peptidyl aldehyde inhibitors of M
                  <jats:sup>Pro</jats:sup>
                  . This finding will be critical to the development of novel antivirals to address the current global emergency of concerning the COVID-19 pandemic.
                </jats:p>
title: The P3 O - Tert -Butyl-Threonine is Key to High Cellular and Antiviral Potency for Aldehyde-Based SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease Inhibitors

reference.unstructured: Cao, W. ; Cho, C.-C. D. ; Geng, Z. Z. ; Ma, X. R. ; Allen, R. ; Shaabani, N. ; Vatansever, E. C. ; Alugubelli, Y. R. ; Ma, Y. ; Ellenburg, W. H. ; Yang, K. S. ; Qiao, Y. ; Ji, H. ; Xu, S. ; Liu, W. R. , Cellular Activities of SARS-CoV-2 Main Protease Inhibitors Reveal Their Unique Characteristics. bioRxiv 2021, 2021.06.08.447613.
